vix.note: VIX at 17.14, up about 4.5 percent as Wednesday's first FOMC under new Chair Kevin Warsh rattled rate expectations (the dot plot now shows nine of eighteen officials penciling in a 2026 hike). It remains subdued in absolute terms and far below the 35 year-high, with Thursday's semis-led rebound keeping fear contained.

macro: Hawkish Fed shock, then a semis-led snapback. Wednesday's first FOMC under Chair Kevin Warsh rattled markets after the dot plot showed nine of eighteen officials now expect a 2026 rate hike, but Thursday June 18 staged a broad rebound: the S&P 500 rose about 1.1 percent, the Nasdaq 1.9 percent, and the semiconductor complex jumped roughly 6 percent. Intel led the tape, soaring 10.6 percent after President Trump said Apple will design and build chips with it in the US, while a US move to end the Iran blockade pushed oil sharply lower. US markets are closed Friday June 19 for the Juneteenth holiday.
action: Favor staged adds in quality AI-infrastructure names (NVDA, AVGO, SMH) on pullbacks rather than chasing the unconfirmed Intel pop; keep oil underweight and hold gold as a hedge into the next Fed signal.

long.h: Intel Soars 10.6% as Trump Says Apple Will Build Chips With It
long.p: President Trump said Apple will design and manufacture chips with Intel in the US, sending Intel up 10.6 percent and its market cap past $600B. Neither company has confirmed terms, but a genuine anchor foundry customer would be a structural catalyst for Intel's turnaround.

long.h: Amazon in Talks to Sell Its Trainium AI Chips to Outside Customers
long.p: AWS has opened discussions to sell its custom Trainium chips to external data-center customers, a business already running above a $20B annual rate. It deepens the custom-silicon threat to Nvidia even as Nvidia's own demand stays strong.

long.h: Google Uses Nvidia's Playbook to Build a Rival AI Chip Business
long.p: Google is wielding its balance sheet to win data-center customers for its in-house TPUs, mirroring Nvidia's ecosystem strategy. The push reinforces the long-term silicon-independence theme among hyperscalers.

long.h: Memory Crisis So Severe Even Apple Cannot Be Safe
long.p: Tim Cook told the WSJ that Apple will raise product prices because of the ongoing memory shortage, underscoring how the AI-driven DRAM squeeze is hitting even the largest buyers. Micron and memory names rallied on the same dynamic.

long.h: Jensen Huang's Comments Signal Nvidia Demand Remains Strong
long.p: Recent remarks from CEO Jensen Huang suggest hardware demand stays robust and Nvidia is far from done capitalizing on the AI buildout, a counter to the AI-derating scare. A new Nvidia-ecosystem ETF also launched, broadening exposure to the buildout.

short.h: Semiconductors Lead Broad Rebound, Group Up Over 6%
short.p: Chip stocks staged a sharp recovery Thursday with the semis complex up roughly 6 percent, Intel up 10.6 percent and Micron nearly 9 percent. The group led the Nasdaq's 1.9 percent bounce off the post-FOMC selloff.

short.h: Fed Dot Plot Shows Nine Officials Now See a 2026 Rate Hike
short.p: Chair Kevin Warsh's first meeting triggered a Wednesday selloff after the dot plot revealed nine of eighteen policymakers now expect rates to rise in 2026. Rate uncertainty lifted the VIX to 17.14 before Thursday's recovery.

short.h: Intel Hires Ex-SK Hynix CEO Seok-Hee Lee for Foundry Packaging
short.p: Intel named industry veteran Seok-Hee Lee EVP of its contract-manufacturing arm to accelerate advanced packaging, reinforcing the foundry-revival narrative alongside the reported Apple tie-up.

short.h: Oil Slides as US Ends Iran Blockade; USO Down to $114.87
short.p: USO has fallen from above $140 in early June to $114.87 as the US moved to end the Iran blockade and OPEC+ supply concerns eased. The geopolitical de-escalation keeps the energy trade on the back foot.

short.h: SpaceX Jumps 37% Since IPO, Adding $674B in Five Days
short.p: SpaceX has gained 37 percent from its $135 offer price in five trading days, adding $673.8B in market cap; Barrons cautions the parabolic move echoes past IPO froth. The mega-listing keeps IPO-supply and merger speculation alive across the complex.

notes.NVDA: Nvidia at $210.69, up about 3 percent in Thursday's semis-led rebound and recovering from the early-week AI-derating scare. Jensen Huang's latest comments point to still-strong hardware demand, though Amazon Trainium and Google TPU competition are a growing structural overhang.
notes.MSFT: Microsoft at $379.40, essentially flat on the bounce and still well off its early-June 428 level as the post-FOMC rate reset weighs on long-duration megacap tech. Azure and AI demand remain the core driver.
notes.INTC: Intel at $133.99, up 10.6 percent after President Trump said Apple will design and build chips with it in the US, lifting market cap past $600B. Terms are unconfirmed; Intel also hired ex-SK Hynix CEO Seok-Hee Lee to lead foundry packaging. High risk until an anchor customer is formalized.
notes.AVGO: Broadcom at $411.35, up about 5 percent with the semis complex; custom-silicon and AI networking demand remain the structural thesis. Still below its early-June 479 print after the AI-spend wobble.
notes.QQQ: Nasdaq-100 ETF at $740.62, up about 2.5 percent as megacap tech and semis led the rebound off the FOMC selloff. Rate-hike risk keeps the index two-way into the next Fed signal.
notes.SMH: Semis ETF at $659.88, up roughly 6 percent in a powerful one-day recovery led by Intel and Micron. The group is the market's high-beta engine; volatility cuts both ways.
notes.SPY: S&P 500 ETF at $746.74, up about 0.8 percent as improving semis breadth offset Fed-driven caution. The index sits just below its early-June highs.
notes.AAPL: Apple at $298.01, up modestly; the reported Intel US-foundry tie-up is a supply-chain positive, but Tim Cook flagged product price hikes as the memory shortage squeezes even Apple. Margin pressure is the near-term watch.
notes.AMZN: Amazon at $244.39, up about 3 percent; AWS is reportedly in talks to sell its Trainium AI chips to outside customers, a business already above a $20B run rate. Self-funded capex and cloud strength keep the medium-term setup intact.
notes.META: Meta at $577.22, up about 2 percent but still well off its $628 peak after an executive-departure report earlier in the week. AI-driven ad targeting is the core driver; sentiment has softened.
notes.GOOGL: Alphabet at $368.03, up about 1 percent; the WSJ reports Google is using its balance sheet to build a rival AI-chip business around its TPUs, reinforcing the silicon-independence story. One of the steadier megacaps here.
notes.TSLA: Tesla at $400.49, up about 1 percent; the SpaceX mega-IPO surge keeps cross-Musk merger speculation alive. EV demand and valuation keep this high-risk despite the narrative.
notes.NFLX: Netflix at $77.38, stabilizing after breaking below its prior 80 to 86 range. Ad tier and live content remain structural positives; near-term momentum is soft.
notes.DIS: Disney at $103.89, up about 3 percent and a relative outperformer; streaming profitability and Parks resilience give it a defensive feel versus high-beta tech.
notes.BRKB: Berkshire at $489.46, easing slightly on a rebound day as expected for the portfolio's ballast. Record cash and a defensive mix keep it the steady anchor as rate uncertainty rises.
notes.DIA: Dow ETF at $515.52, roughly flat; value and broad-market participation cushion the tech-led swings. Less exposed to the AI-derating debate than QQQ.
notes.OIL: USO at $114.87, down hard from above $140 in early June as the US moved to end the Iran blockade and OPEC+ supply concerns weighed. Underweight stance intact on the geopolitical de-escalation.
notes.BTC: Bitcoin near $62,900 (June 18 close), down about 2 percent on the week as the hawkish Fed lifts the dollar and real-rate expectations. Holding the low-60s with risk-off pressure persisting.
notes.GOLD: Gold at $4,246 (June 18), down about 3 percent as the post-FOMC dollar and real-rate bounce pressured the metal; still a core hedge but well below the early-June 4,505 high.
notes.SILVER: Silver at $66.32 (June 18), down sharply (about 6 percent) alongside gold and its industrial-demand leg; higher volatility than gold makes it the more aggressive hedge.
